narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive dedication in pursuing this path despite the unique challenges many women of color face in competitive academic fields. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.70 stands out well above the typical student's average of 3.21, reflecting your strong performance in college, while your LSAT score of 31 is below the usual 36.70, and your family comes from a middle-income bracket. You ranked in the 2nd decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, compared to averages around the 5th or 6th decile for most students, and you've indicated plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of applicants. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 67.4% chance that you would fail it—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to succeed right away. The biggest factors pushing toward this failure prediction were your lower year-one and year-three school decile rankings, which had the strongest negative influence by signaling to the model that your high school-level performance might not translate to bar success, unlike typical students who rank higher. On a positive note, your excellent undergraduate GPA strongly pulled the prediction toward passing, countering some of those concerns more effectively than it does for most applicants. Your LSAT score added a modest push toward failure, while your middle-income background and full-time plans offered small boosts toward success, aligning with common profiles. Compared to typical students, your GPA is a standout strength that many lack, but your decile rankings weigh more heavily in the model's eyes, tipping the balance against you. This doesn't define your potential—many applicants with similar profiles improve through targeted preparation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking mentorship programs tailored for Black women in law to strengthen your academic deciles, and reapplying with updated credentials that highlight your resilience. You've already accomplished more than most with your GPA; with focused steps, you can shift this prediction in your favor and open those doors. Keep pushing—you've got this.
